critic_raw: The replication study of 'Fairness and Bias in Online Selection' raises several methodological concerns. First, the definition of 'perfect fairness' as balanced selection with respect to sensitive attributes is operationalized without a rigorous mathematical formulation — the claim that algorithms are 'perfectly fair' hinges on a strict demographic parity definition, but the paper does not establish whether this fairness criterion is the appropriate one for all contexts, nor does it compare against other fairness notions (equalized odds, individual fairness). Second, the performance metric — probability of selecting the optimal candidate (Secretary) and average value of the selected candidate (Prophet) — conflates fairness constraints with optimality in a way that is not disentangled: it is unclear whether fairness-constrained performance is being compared fairly to unconstrained baselines that are not subject to the same restrictions. Third, the experimental design relies on particular datasets whose distributional properties (group sizes, candidate value distributions) directly determine whether the proposed algorithms can achieve both fairness and near-optimal performance simultaneously; no sensitivity analysis is provided to show robustness across different distributional regimes. Fourth, the replication involves recreating algorithms and experiments from scratch without access to the original authors' code, introducing uncontrolled implementation variability that could explain any discrepancies between replicated and original results.
all_issues_raised: Perfect fairness is defined via demographic parity without justification that this criterion is appropriate across all contexts; other fairness notions are not discussed.
Performance comparisons conflate fairness-constrained and unconstrained algorithms without explicit isolation of the fairness penalty on performance.
Experimental results depend on dataset distributional properties (group sizes, value distributions) without sensitivity analysis across different distributional regimes.
Replication from scratch without original code introduces implementation variability that confounds attribution of any result discrepancies.

round_transcripts.critic: The replication study of 'Fairness and Bias in Online Selection' raises several methodological concerns. First, the definition of 'perfect fairness' as balanced selection with respect to sensitive attributes is operationalized without a rigorous mathematical formulation — the claim that algorithms are 'perfectly fair' hinges on a strict demographic parity definition, but the paper does not establish whether this fairness criterion is the appropriate one for all contexts, nor does it compare against other fairness notions (equalized odds, individual fairness). Second, the performance metric — probability of selecting the optimal candidate (Secretary) and average value of the selected candidate (Prophet) — conflates fairness constraints with optimality in a way that is not disentangled: it is unclear whether fairness-constrained performance is being compared fairly to unconstrained baselines that are not subject to the same restrictions. Third, the experimental design relies on particular datasets whose distributional properties (group sizes, candidate value distributions) directly determine whether the proposed algorithms can achieve both fairness and near-optimal performance simultaneously; no sensitivity analysis is provided to show robustness across different distributional regimes. Fourth, the replication involves recreating algorithms from scratch without access to the original code, introducing uncontrolled implementation variability.
round_transcripts.defender: The critic raises legitimate concerns but several are mitigated by the nature of the original and replication work. On fairness criterion choice: the original paper explicitly targets demographic parity (proportional representation) because the Secretary and Prophet problems are HR-inspired — in hiring, demographic balance is the legally and ethically relevant notion, making alternative fairness criteria secondary. The replication appropriately reproduces this choice without substituting its own judgment. On the comparison of fairness-constrained vs. unconstrained baselines: this is standard in the fairness literature; comparing constrained to unconstrained algorithms highlights the efficiency-fairness trade-off, which is the central claim. The paper does not claim the fair algorithms dominate unconstrained ones in absolute performance — it claims near-parity with added fairness guarantees. On distributional sensitivity: the use of multiple real-world datasets with varying group proportions provides some empirical coverage of distributional variation, even absent formal sensitivity analysis. On implementation variability from scratch: this is an acknowledged limitation of replication studies generally, and is addressed by checking whether key algorithmic properties (proportionality guarantees, convergence) hold in the replicated implementation before running full experiments.

round_transcripts.critic: The defense does not fully resolve the core concerns. Accepting that demographic parity is the target criterion still leaves open whether the 'perfect fairness' label is justified — demographic parity can be trivially achieved at the cost of extreme performance degradation (e.g., random selection), so the interesting claim is the degree of performance retention under the fairness constraint. The defense acknowledges the efficiency-fairness trade-off but does not address whether the reported near-parity results are stable across the full range of group-size imbalances found in real applications. The use of multiple datasets provides breadth but not a systematic sensitivity analysis — results could be cherry-picked if only favorable distributional regimes are represented. The implementation variability issue remains: checking algorithmic properties does not guarantee that numerical outcomes match the original, and without the original code, discrepancies in reported performance cannot be definitively attributed to methodological differences versus coding choices.
round_transcripts.defender: The remaining concerns are real but are characteristic limitations of the replication study genre rather than fatal flaws in this specific work. The performance-under-constraint analysis is present in the original paper in the form of direct comparisons across baseline and proposed algorithms on the same datasets; the replication inherits this framing. The dataset selection criticism — potential cherry-picking of favorable distributions — would require evidence of omitted unfavorable experiments, which is not established. Implementation variability is an inherent challenge for all replication studies without source code; the contribution of this type of study is precisely to assess reproducibility under realistic conditions (access only to pseudocode/paper descriptions). The claim of 'perfect fairness' is a theoretical guarantee of the algorithm under stated assumptions, not an empirical claim requiring sensitivity analysis across all distributions — this distinction is important and the critic conflates the two.

all_issues_adjudicated: No formal sensitivity analysis across distributional regimes — the use of multiple datasets is not equivalent to a systematic sweep of group-size imbalances.
Implementation variability from scratch replication remains an uncontrolled confounder for any result discrepancies.
